result: When I was twelve, time crawled like a garden snail over wet concrete; summer vacations stretched so wide I could lie down inside-to-chin across two empty months and still have space left for boredom to bloom. Now in my thirties the same two months slip past like subway doors closing before I can finish chewing a thought. This elastic fraud—time pretending to contract while we shove more memories through—is maybe the universe’s kindest cruelty. It keeps shrinking the calendar so we grip tighter to what sprouts: the cinnamon smell of seven a.m. toast lifted to a child’s stubborn curls, the hush of streetlights after rain, the way my father’s hands tremble now when he offers me tea, a handshake apology for decades of silence brewed between mugs. These grains refuse the hourglass. They glom together in pockets of emotional amber, hardening into a geology I can crack open in sudden supermarket aisles. Yesterday I paused beside the cilantro because a stranger laughed like my grandmother used to—sharp, fluted, incredulous—and suddenly I am seven again, stealing tamarind candies from her tin while she pretends not to notice. Such moments rear up like unexpected wildlife. Best to keep still, let them sniff our palms, accept the soft nose-bump before they vanish again. The clock can race all it wants; the amber keeps its own slow archive. Maybe that’s enough inheritance: to scatter ourselves forward, fossilized sweetness, into someone else’s unguarded Wednesday afternoon.
